summary:
The Plague of Justinian (541–549 AD, with recurrences through 750 AD) was the first recorded pandemic of bubonic plague — caused by the bacterium Yersinia pestis — and one of the most catastrophic disease events in world history, killing an estimated 25–50 million people (possibly 25–60% of Europe's population) across the Byzantine Empire, the Mediterranean world, and the Middle East over roughly two centuries of recurrent outbreaks. The plague reached Constantinople in 541 AD during the reign of [[event:2119|Justinian I]], where the historian Procopius recorded it killing up to 10,000 people per day at its peak.

The Plague of Justinian had profound geopolitical consequences. It struck the Byzantine Empire during the critical period of [[event:2903|Justinian's reconquest]] campaigns, killing soldiers and civilians alike, disrupting agricultural production and tax collection, and fundamentally weakening both Byzantine and Sassanid Persian capacity to resist the subsequent Islamic expansion. Some historians argue the plague was a decisive factor in the Islamic conquests' speed and completeness: the Arab armies that swept through Syria, Egypt, and Persia in the 630s–640s were attacking societies severely weakened by a century of recurring plague mortality. The plague's origins in the Ethiopian highlands or Central Asian rodent reservoirs, its spread via the grain trade routes connecting Egypt with Constantinople and the Western Mediterranean, and its recurrence over 200 years all followed patterns that would be repeated — with even more devastating effect — by the Black Death in the 14th century. DNA analysis of plague victims from early medieval burial sites has confirmed the Justinianic plague as bubonic plague, establishing the ancient origins of one of history's most feared diseases.

read_more_links.title: Yersinia pestis bubonic plague ancient DNA
